title: Lung Development and Maturation
authors: Kallapur SG, Jobe AH / Fanaroff & Martin 2025

blocks.text:
ทำไม neonatologist ต้องเข้าใจ Lung Development and Maturation?
ปอดทารกไม่ใช่แค่ 'ปอดเล็ก' — มันกำลังสร้างตัวเองตั้งแต่ GA 3 สัปดาห์จนถึงอายุ 20 ปี และทุกการตัดสินใจของเราในห้อง delivery หรือ NICU ส่งผลต่อ trajectory นั้น. (1) Viability เกิดขึ้นที่ canalicular stage (16–25 wk) — นี่คือจุดที่ air-blood barrier เริ่มก่อตัวและ surfactant synthesis เริ่มต้น; (2) Antenatal corticosteroids (Liggins & Howie 1972) และ postnatal surfactant (Fujiwara 1980) คือ 2 interventions ที่ลด RDS mortality มากที่สุดในประวัติ neonatology; (3) Mechanical ventilation ของ saccular lung → disrupts elastin at alveolar crests → arrested alveolar development → BPD; (4) SP-B deficiency เป็น lethal congenital lung disease — ทารก term แต่หายใจล้มเหลวตั้งแต่แรกเกิด; ABCA3 deficiency พบบ่อยกว่า; (5) Chorioamnionitis → fetal lung maturation ผ่าน IL-1/LPS → additive กับ corticosteroids. เข้าใจ lung development = เข้าใจว่าเราช่วยหรือทำร้ายปอดทารกอยู่.
เป้าหมายการเรียนรู้วันนี้
ระบุ 5 stages ของ lung development พร้อม gestational week, event หลัก และ clinical significance ของแต่ละ stage
อธิบาย canalicular stage (16–25 wk): 3 major events ที่ทำให้ปอด potentially viable — acinus formation, air-blood barrier, surfactant synthesis
อธิบาย alveolarization: timing (32 wk – อายุ 20 ปี), alveolar numbers (term: 50–150M; adult: 500M), factors ที่ delay หรือ stimulate
อธิบาย fetal lung fluid: composition, production rate (4–5 mL/kg/hr), clearance mechanism (ENaC, epinephrine), clinical relevance (TTN)
จำแนก congenital lung malformations: CCAM, bronchogenic cyst, congenital lobar emphysema, pulmonary sequestration — ลักษณะ, สถานที่, blood supply
ระบุ lethal pulmonary disorders: pulmonary hypoplasia (causes), alveolar capillary dysplasia (FOXF1), congenital acinar dysplasia (TBX4/FGF10/FGFR2) — presentation และ prognosis
อธิบาย surfactant composition: 4 SP proteins (A, B, C, D) — water-soluble vs hydrophobic, functions, genetic deficiency syndromes
อธิบาย surfactant synthesis/secretion: type II cell, lamellar body, tubular myelin → surface film; pool sizes ใน RDS vs term (~5 mg/kg vs ~100 mg/kg)
อธิบาย physiologic effects ของ surfactant: alveolar stability, opening pressure, pressure-volume curve, deflation stability
อธิบาย glucocorticoid effects บน fetal lung: structural maturation (surface area ↑, ↓ edema tendency), ↑ ENaC, additive กับ surfactant, repeat dose controversies
อธิบาย chorioamnionitis → induced lung maturation: mechanism (direct fetal contact via IL-1/LPS), additive กับ corticosteroids, ruptured membranes ≠ contraindication to steroids
1. Lung Structural Development — 5 Stages
1.1 ภาพรวม 5 Stages

rows: Lung bud off foregut → dichotomous branching; vasculature from 6th aortic arch; lobar airways by 37 days; segmental by 42 days; failure → TEF, aplasia
15–20 generations airway branching complete; cuboidal glycogen-rich epithelium; cartilage 9–10 wk; ciliated/goblet/basal cells 13 wk; arteries to principal pathways by 14 wk
VIABILITY TRANSITION: acinus formation, double→single capillary fusion (air-blood barrier), type II cells + lamellar bodies, surfactant synthesis; capillary dysplasia if double network fails to fuse
Terminal sacs elongate/branch/widen; shallow alveolar crests with elastin by 28 wk; 50–150M alveoli at term; alveolarization begins ~32 wk; most rapid growth 32 wk – first months postnatal
Secondary crest formation (elastin-tipped); alveoli 50–150M at term → 500M adult; alveolarization continues to age 20 (Narayanan AJRCCM 2012); catch-up possible in mild BPD

blocks.text: 1.2 Transcription Factors และ Regulators

Embryonic stage: FOXF1, TTF-1 (thyroid transcription factor-1), FOXA1, FOXA2, GATA6 → epithelial lineage commitment; ≥15 HOX genes; FGF-7, FGF-10 (from mesenchyme); VEGF → vascular plexus; loss of FOXF1 → alveolar capillary dysplasia

Canalicular epithelial differentiation: TTF-1, FOXa1, FOXa2, GATA6 → type II cell; glycogen in type II cells → substrate for surfactant; type I cells derived from type II or intermediary cells; type I cells = 93% alveolar surface (area), but only 8% of lung cells by number
Type II cells (16% of lung cells): 7% of alveolar surface area but produce ALL surfactant; lamellar bodies first appear 20–24 wk
1.3 Alveolarization — What Can Interrupt It

Modulators of Alveolarization — จำ 2 lists
ปัจจัยที่ ขัดขวาง alveolarization: (1) mechanical ventilation — disrupts elastin distribution at developing crests → fewer larger alveoli ('arrested development' morphology, not fibrosis); (2) antenatal AND postnatal glucocorticoids — thin interstitium, delay secondary crest formation; (3) hyperoxia หรือ hypoxia; (4) poor nutrition; (5) pro-inflammatory mediators (↑ in chorioamnionitis model); (6) chorioamnionitis (in sheep) — delay alveolar + microvascular development. ปัจจัยที่ STIMULATE alveolarization: (1) Vitamin A (retinoids) — กลไก: retinoid receptors on fibroblasts and type II cells; (2) thyroxine (T4). Good news: alveolarization continues to age ~20 (Narayanan AJRCCM 2012 using ³He MRI); catch-up alveolarization demonstrated in ex-preterm children with mild BPD at 10–14 yr (Narayanan AJRCCM 2013). อย่างไรก็ตาม: preterm infants have ↓ FEV1 trajectory at birth → ↑ risk early-onset COPD in adulthood.
2. Fetal Lung Fluid
2.1 Composition and Production
Volume: ~40 mL/kg body weight at term — slightly larger than FRC after air breathing established

Composition (sheep model, most mammalian species): high Cl⁻ (157 mEq/L), low HCO₃⁻, low protein — maintained by transepithelial Cl⁻ secretion + HCO₃⁻ reabsorption; rhesus monkey values ≠ sheep (species difference)
Production rate: 4–5 mL/kg/hr → ~400 mL/day in 3–4 kg fetus; intrinsic metabolic function of lung epithelium (not dependent on vascular hydrostatic pressure or fetal breathing movements)
Outflow: tracheal pressure exceeds amniotic fluid pressure by ~2 mm Hg; fluid flows out during fetal breathing → pharynx → swallowed or into amniotic fluid
2.2 Clearance at Birth
Active labor: fetal lung fluid volume ↓ before delivery; ~35% remains to be absorbed with first breaths
Mechanism: most fluid → interstitial spaces → pulmonary vasculature; <20% via lymphatics; interstitial clearance takes HOURS

Molecular switch: ENaC (epithelial sodium channel) — Na⁺ active transport reverses secretion to absorption; genetic ablation of ENaC subunits → death in newborn mice (lung fluid retention); glucocorticoids upregulate ENaC mRNA in fetal human lung

Epinephrine: completely stops fetal lung fluid production at concentrations seen during labor; epinephrine-mediated reversal NOT present in preterm lung but CAN be induced by pretreatment with corticosteroid + T3 → explains why preterm infants are more prone to TTN
Indomethacin: ↓ fetal lung fluid production AND fetal urine production (both prostaglandin-dependent)
TTN (transient tachypnea): delayed clearance of fetal lung fluid; ↑ risk with C-section (no labor → no epinephrine surge), preterm birth, maternal sedation
3. Disorders of Lung Development
3.1 Congenital Malformations (Non-Lethal Group)

rows: Multicystic overproliferation of terminal respiratory bronchioles; no normal alveoli; intrapulmonary; maintains communication with tracheobronchial tree; normal blood supply; unilobar + unilateral; Stocker 5-type classification by cyst size/epithelium
Usually detected prenatally on US; polyhydramnios + hydrops if large; postnatal resection if symptomatic or type 0/1; risk of malignant transformation (pleuropulmonary blastoma)
Single unilocular cyst (fluid/mucous); lined by pseudostratified ciliated columnar epithelium + cartilage; mediastinum most common; lung parenchyma cysts: NO communication with tracheobronchial tree
Respiratory distress if airway compressed; recurrent pneumonia; surgical resection
Hyperinflation of 1+ pulmonary lobes → compresses surrounding structures; mediastinal shift possible; left upper or right middle lobe most common
Differential: check ball-valve obstruction (cartilage deficiency, mucous plug, extrinsic compression); surgical resection if severe

Lung tissue isolated from neighboring lung; NO communication with bronchial tree; systemic artery supply; intralobar (childhood diagnosis) vs extralobar (prenatal diagnosis, own pleura, systemic venous drainage)
Prenatal: usually extralobar; postnatal: intralobar presents with recurrent infection; treatment: embolization or surgical resection

blocks.text: 3.2 Pulmonary Hypoplasia
Definition: ↓ lung size + cell number + narrowed airways + delayed epithelial differentiation + surfactant deficiency; severity correlates with timing and duration of causative factor
Primary hypoplasia: abnormal TTF-1 or FGF family members → abnormal morphogenesis; rare; severe forms = acinar aplasia (often lethal)

Secondary Pulmonary Hypoplasia — Clinical Causes (จำเป็น exam )
Thoracic compression: renal agenesis/Potter syndrome (oligohydramnios), urinary tract outflow obstruction, oligohydramnios <28 wk from PPROM, extra-amniotic fetal development. Decreased intrathoracic space: CDH (ipsilateral > contralateral; both may be hypoplastic), pleural effusions, abdominal distension, thoracic dystrophies, intrathoracic masses. Decreased fetal breathing: intrauterine CNS damage, Werdnig-Hoffmann (SMA type 1), other neuropathies/myopathies. Other: primary hypoplasia, trisomy 21, multiple congenital anomalies, erythroblastosis fetalis. CDH special case: tracheal occlusion (FETO) in late gestation can reverse hypoplasia by increasing lung fluid retention → stretch → ↑ branching morphogenesis; BUT occlusion → ↓ type II cells + surfactant deficiency → must be reversed at delivery.
3.3 Lethal Pulmonary Disorders

rows: FOXF1 (16q24.1) mutations — majority; abnormal capillary development around alveoli; misaligned pulmonary veins located WITH pulmonary artery in same adventitial sheath
Severe refractory PPHN; 60–80% have extrapulmonary abnormalities; unresponsive to all therapy; uniformly fatal without transplant
TBX4, FGF10, FGFR2 mutations; maturational arrest at canalicular → early saccular stage; bilateral diffuse acinar impairment
Refractory respiratory failure + PPHN; cardiac + renal anomalies in some; fatal within weeks to rarely months
Maturational arrest canalicular → early saccular; likely regulatory gene mutations
Respiratory failure + PPHN; poor response to medical management
TTF-1, FGF10/FGFR2 pathway mutations; severe acinar aplasia
Often lethal; depends on severity; may overlap with CAD spectrum

blocks.text: 4. Alveolar Macrophages ใน Fetal and Neonatal Lung
Normal fetal lung: ไม่มี alveolar macrophages; non-human primates + sheep: very few macrophages in fetal lung

Postnatally: alveolar macrophages begin to populate with onset of air breathing; derived from circulating monocytes; differentiated by GM-CSF + transcription factor PU.1; once differentiated → long lifespan, do not recycle
Functions: immune surveillance, phagocytosis, antigen presentation, interaction with adaptive immune cells, surfactant homeostasis (clear excess surfactant from airspaces)

Chorioamnionitis: infection in amniotic fluid → matures fetal lung macrophages + stimulates migration into fetal alveolar spaces BEFORE birth → 'primed' alveolar macrophages at delivery → ↑ inflammatory response → can contribute to BPD
5. Surfactant Metabolism — Composition
5.1 Lipid Composition
Surfactant = 70–80% phospholipids + ~8% protein + ~10% neutral lipids (primarily cholesterol)

Saturated phosphatidylcholine (sat-PC): ~50% of total surfactant; BOTH fatty acids = palmitic acid (C16:0); principal surface-active component; used as marker of surfactant metabolism (stable isotope studies)

Phosphatidylglycerol (PG): 4–15% depending on species; ↑ PG = lung maturity marker; immature lung: ↑ phosphatidylinositol (PI) → as lung matures PI ↓ and PG ↑; PG in amniotic fluid → lung maturity test

Immature vs mature surfactant (Table 63.1): immature: ↑ glycogen in type II cells, few lamellar bodies, low sat-PC/total-PC ratio (0.6 vs 0.7 mature), PI >10% vs 2%, PG <1% vs ~10%, ↓ SP-A, ↓ function
5.2 Surfactant Proteins — 4 SPs

SP-A, SP-B, SP-C, SP-D — จำครบ
SP-A (24 kDa → 36 kDa glycosylated; collectin; water-soluble): collagen domain → triple helix → 650 kDa multimer; ↓ protein-mediated inhibition of surfactant; forms TUBULAR MYELIN with SP-B + phospholipids; major function: innate host defense (binds GBS, S. aureus, HSV-1, influenza); ↓ SP-A in preterm lung; ↑ with corticosteroid; SP-A polymorphisms → ↑ RDS, BPD, viral bronchiolitis risk; NOT in surfactant treatment preparations. SP-B (40 kDa precursor → 8 kDa; hydrophobic; organic-solvent-extractable): facilitates surface absorption + low surface tensions; co-secreted with phospholipids from lamellar bodies; genetic absence (121ins2 frameshift → most common) = LETHAL respiratory failure at term birth; absence also causes loss of SP-C + abnormal lamellar bodies; pro-SP-C forms = diagnostic marker of SP-B deficiency; mice with <20% normal SP-B → respiratory failure. SP-C (22 kDa precursor → 4 kDa; extremely hydrophobic; organic-solvent-extractable): mRNA present from early gestation → restricted to type II cells with maturation; mice lacking SP-C: normal at birth but develop progressive ILD + emphysema; humans: SP-C mutations (dominant negative in processing) → familial ILD at variable ages. SP-B + SP-C work cooperatively for rapid adsorption + low surface tension. SP-D (43 kDa; collectin; hydrophilic; water-soluble): structural similarity to SP-A; lectin-like function; synthesized by type II + Clara cells; ↓ SP-D in mice → ↑ surfactant lipid pools + emphysema; SP-D added to surfactant treatment → ↓ ventilator-mediated inflammation (Sato AJRCCM 2010).
5.3 Genetic Defects in Surfactant System
SP-B deficiency: ~14% of term infants with severe RDS (>300 term infants study); 121ins2 = most common mutation; autosomal recessive; diagnosis: pro-SP-C forms in airway samples

ABCA3 deficiency: more common than SP-B deficiency; ABCA3 gene product localized to lamellar bodies, functions as lipid transporter; clinical presentation identical to SP-B deficiency; autosomal recessive; some mutations → milder disease with chronic ILD
SP-C deficiency: usually dominant negative processing mutations; progressive ILD at variable age onset (not purely neonatal)
Some lethal RDS in term infants = identified mutations; other infants likely have yet-to-be-identified mutations
6. Surfactant Synthesis, Pool Sizes, and Physiology
6.1 Synthesis and Secretion
Type II cell = site of all surfactant metabolism; phospholipid synthesis in ER using glucose, phosphate, fatty acids; condensation with SP-B + SP-C forms lamellar body lipoprotein complex

Stimulators of surfactant secretion: (1) beta-agonists (potent); (2) purines (ATP, adenosine); (3) mechanical stretch (lung distention, hyperventilation) — explains secretion with first breath and during labor

Time course: de novo synthesis to lamellar body: long delay (ER → Golgi → lamellar body); ¹³C-labeled glucose → PC detected in airway samples ~20 hr; peak enrichment ~70 hr → explains why RDS clinical course = 3–5 days
6.2 Surfactant Pool Sizes
RDS infants: ~5 mg/kg surfactant pool; preterm lambs can be managed with CPAP if pool >4 mg/kg (Mulrooney 2005)
Term infants: ~100 mg/kg; large amniotic fluid surfactant pool confirms large fetal pool at term
Recovery from RDS (preterm monkeys): pool size increases to ~100 mg/kg within 3–4 days; saturated PC in airway samples of human RDS infants → normalizes by 4–7 days (Hallman 1991)

Recycling: ~90% of phospholipids recycled intact (endocytosis → multivesicular bodies → lamellar bodies → re-secretion); adult: only ~50% efficient; slow catabolism = clinical benefit of exogenous surfactant treatment (dose remains in lung days)

Exogenous surfactant kinetics: does NOT feedback-inhibit endogenous synthesis; half-life of treatment dose = several days; taken up by type II cells → transforms poor-function treatment surfactant into better surfactant (Ueda 1994)
6.3 Alveolar Life Cycle of Surfactant
Lamellar bodies → 'unravel' → tubular myelin: SP-A at corners of lattice structure; requires SP-A + SP-B + phospholipids; tubular myelin = reserve pool in hypophase
Surface film formation: tubular myelin + other lipoprotein arrays → surface film; area compression squeezes out unsaturated lipid → concentrated sat-PC in film
'Used' surfactant leaves as small vesicles (little SP-A, SP-B, SP-C) → recycled to type II cells

Equilibrium: ~50% surface-active forms (tubular myelin + large aggregates) + ~50% small vesicles (inactive, recycling pool); in preterm: faster conversion surface-active → inactive; pulmonary edema accelerates this → depletes active fraction despite normal total pool
6.4 Physiologic Effects — Pressure-Volume Curves

Surfactant Effects on Surfactant-Deficient Preterm Lung
Opening pressure: surfactant-deficient preterm rabbit lungs: inflation starts only at >25 cm H₂O; after surfactant treatment: ↓ to ~15 cm H₂O (lower surface tension in fluid menisci). Maximal volume: ↑ ~2.5-fold at 35 cm H₂O → recruits alveolar gas volume for gas exchange; without surfactant, opening pressures of distal units >35 cm H₂O → exceed rupture pressure → air leak risk. Deflation stability: surfactant-deficient lung collapses at low transpulmonary pressures; surfactant-treated lung retains ~40% volume at 5 cm H₂O (= static FRC equivalent). Dynamic mechanics: longer expiratory time constant after surfactant → ↑ FRC by 2 mechanisms: (1) improved deflation stability; (2) longer expiratory time. Clinical observation: initial surfactant response = rapid ↑ oxygenation (↑ lung volume) → Pco₂ and compliance improve more gradually.
7. Lung Maturation — Spontaneous และ Induced
7.1 Surfactant Appearance with Development
Lamellar bodies first appear: 20–24 wk in human fetal type II cells
Lung maturity (no RDS): generally present after 35 wk normal gestation — but 12-week window of potential early maturation exists because surfactant machinery is inducible early

Lung maturity tests (amniotic fluid): L/S ratio (lecithin:sphingomyelin; Gluck 1971); ↑ PG (Hallman 1976); bubble stability test; lamellar body count; commercial tests; now rarely used in obstetric practice — decisions based on clinical assessment; ACS used routinely when preterm delivery likely
7.2 Glucocorticoids and Lung Maturation
Liggins & Howie 1972 (Pediatrics 50:515): first RCT showing maternal corticosteroids ↓ RDS incidence in preterm infants — landmark study

Mechanisms (multiple): (1) structural: ↑ surface area for gas exchange, thinning of interstitium; (2) ↑ type II cell maturation (glycogen loss, ↑ fatty acid synthesis, ↑ beta-receptors, ↑ choline incorporation into sat-PC); (3) ↓ pulmonary edema tendency; (4) ↑ ENaC (mRNA) → ↑ fluid clearance; (5) ↑ SP-A levels

Surfactant pool timing: surfactant pool sizes do NOT increase until >4 days after maternal glucocorticoid in sheep — lung function improves within 24h via structural changes even before surfactant increases

Site of action in late gestation: primarily lung mesenchyme; glucocorticoids → ↑ matrix fibroblast differentiation from mesenchymal progenitors; matrix fibroblasts mature type II cells via ECM interactions

Additive effect with surfactant: ACS + postnatal surfactant → larger lung gas volumes than either alone (Ikegami 1991); corticosteroid changes dose-response curve so less surfactant needed for same response
ACS benefit beyond lung: ↓ PDA, ↓ IVH, ↓ NEC, ↑ kidney tubular function, ↑ postnatal blood pressure (multi-organ effect)

Repeat doses: given at 7–10 day intervals; maternal betamethasone at 7-day intervals in sheep → fetal growth restriction but augmented lung maturation; RCTs show modest benefit; concern about ≥4 courses; benefits for decreased RDS mortality very modest for repeat doses; possible adverse neuropsychiatric effects at or close to term (Ninan 2022 JAMA Pediatr)

TRH (thyrotropin-releasing hormone): thyroid hormones synergize with corticosteroids in vitro; TRH crosses placenta → ↑ fetal thyroid; large RCTs → NO benefit + possible risks (ACTOBAT 1995 Lancet) → NOT recommended
7.3 Intrauterine Infection and Lung Maturation
Chorioamnionitis association: chronic infection + histologic chorioamnionitis frequent in preterm labor 22–30 wk → associated with decreased incidence of RDS

Experimental model: intra-amniotic endotoxin or IL-1 → more consistent and longer lung maturation than maternal corticosteroids; direct fetal lung contact → modest lung inflammation/injury → resolves with ↑ surfactant lipid + protein pools + improved lung mechanics (Kuypers 2012 Am J Physiol)

Additive with ACS: chorioamnionitis + maternal corticosteroids → additive lung maturation (Newnham 2001); corticosteroids suppress inflammation initially but amplify later → suggests maturation via innate immune priming
Clinical implication: PPROM (surrogate for chorioamnionitis) is NOT a contraindication to maternal corticosteroid treatment (Harding 2001)
Concept: infant with RDS = 'normal unstressed preterm'; preterm WITHOUT RDS = has experienced stress sufficient to induce lung maturation
8. NICU Practical — Lung Development in Daily Care
8.1 Optimizing Surfactant Therapy
Indication: RDS with need for FiO₂ >0.30–0.40 on CPAP or any ventilation; natural surfactants (contain SP-B + SP-C) > protein-free synthetic for in vivo function
Timing: early rescue (within 2h) vs prophylactic; INSURE (INtubate-SURfactant-Extubate) or LISA (Less Invasive Surfactant Administration) via thin catheter during CPAP — avoid prolonged intubation

Dose: most natural surfactants: 100–200 mg/kg (phospholipid content); re-dose if FiO₂ increases >0.30–0.40 after initial response; ≤3 doses; repeat doses show diminishing returns due to ↑ endogenous surfactant

Why CPAP works in mild RDS: maintains FRC → keeps alveoli open between breaths → ↓ surfactant consumption (prevents de-recruitment); Mulrooney (2005) showed preterm lambs with pool >4 mg/kg can be managed with CPAP alone
Alveolar stability principle: surfactant ↓ opening pressure (25 → 15 cm H₂O in rabbit model) + ↑ maximal volume + ↑ deflation stability → more uniform inflation → less air leak + overdistension
8.2 Antenatal Corticosteroids — What Residents Must Know

Standard: betamethasone 12 mg IM × 2 doses, 24h apart (dexamethasone alternative); give if preterm delivery anticipated 24h–7 days from 23+0 to 34+6 wk (per ACOG PB 171, 2021 update); consider 22+0–22+6 based on local viability practice
Benefit window: optimal benefit 24h–7 days after first dose; some benefit even <24h; benefit may diminish after 7 days but repeat course guidance evolving
Rescue course: single rescue course if >7 days since prior course and still at risk; ≥4 courses: concern about growth restriction + possible neurodevelopmental effects

Late preterm (34–36 wk): betamethasone × 1 course reduces RDS and respiratory morbidity (ALPS trial, Gyamfi-Bannerman NEJM 2016); BUT at term: possible adverse neuropsychiatric effects — narrow benefit-risk window; new concern for ↑ behavioral disorders (Ninan JAMA Pediatr 2022) needs context (is it the indication, not the steroid?)
8.3 Recognizing Lethal Lung Disorders at Delivery

Refractory PPHN in term infant + extrapulmonary anomalies: consider ACD (FOXF1 mutation) — does NOT respond to iNO, sildenafil, ECMO (unless bridge to transplant); confirm by lung biopsy (misaligned PVs) + FOXF1 sequencing

Term RDS + family history: consider SP-B deficiency (121ins2) or ABCA3 mutation; send BAL or tracheal aspirate for pro-SP-C forms (marker of SP-B deficiency); surfactant treatment = bridge while diagnosis made; long-term: lung transplant or sirolimus (case reports for ABCA3)

CDH + pulmonary hypoplasia: surfactant NOT routinely recommended (surfactant deficiency is secondary; inflammatory mediators + structural abnormalities dominate); gentle ventilation; ↑ PEEP caution (over-distends contralateral lung); consider early iNO for PPHN
8.4 Protecting Alveolarization

Minimize mechanical ventilation: non-invasive respiratory support (CPAP, NIPPV, HFNC) as primary strategy; if intubated → wean to extubation as fast as possible; target 'gentle ventilation' tidal volumes 4–5 mL/kg
Avoid unnecessary postnatal steroids: dexamethasone for BPD → ↑ CP risk (DART trial); use only when benefit > risk; budesonide intratracheal reduces BPD but watch neurologic outcomes
Vitamin A supplementation: 5000 IU IM 3×/week × 4 weeks in ELBW: ↓ BPD (OR 0.87, Tyson et al NEJM 1999); stimulates alveolarization via retinoid receptors; limited availability in some countries
Optimize nutrition: poor nutrition → ↓ alveolarization; early aggressive parenteral nutrition + early enteral feeds; target: no growth restriction in NICU
9. 5 Bedside Pearls

PEARL 1 — Canalicular Stage GW 16–25: จุดเปลี่ยนสู่ Viability
Canalicular stage (GW 16–25) คือจุดที่ปอดเปลี่ยนจาก non-viable → potentially viable: 3 events พร้อมกัน — (1) acinus formation; (2) double capillary network fuses → single capillary = air-blood barrier; (3) type II cells + lamellar bodies → surfactant synthesis. ถ้า double capillary network ล้มเหลวที่จะ fuse → alveolar capillary dysplasia → severe hypoxemia. ที่ GA 22–23 wk บางคนมี lung structure พอสำหรับ gas exchange → นี่คือเหตุที่ ACS ช่วยได้แม้ที่ GA นั้น. หลักการ: คิด canalicular stage ทุกครั้งที่ตัดสินใจ viability.

PEARL 2 — SP-B Deficiency: Term Baby Dying of RDS
ทุกครั้งที่เจอ term baby ที่มี refractory RDS โดยไม่มี explanation ชัดเจน — คิดถึง SP-B deficiency (121ins2 frameshift mutation = most common) หรือ ABCA3 deficiency. SP-B deficiency เป็น lethal: ไม่มี SP-B → lamellar bodies abnormal + SP-C ไม่ processed correctly → respiratory failure ตั้งแต่แรกเกิดแม้เป็น term. Diagnostic clue: pro-SP-C forms ใน tracheal aspirate = marker of SP-B deficiency. ABCA3 deficiency พบบ่อยกว่า (lipid transporter ใน lamellar body) → clinical presentation เหมือน SP-B deficiency. Treatment: lung transplantation; surfactant = bridge therapy only.

PEARL 3 — Mechanical Ventilation ทำลาย Elastin ที่ Developing Alveolar Crests
Mechanical ventilation ของ saccular lung → disrupts elastin distribution: ปกติ elastin อยู่ที่ tips ของ developing alveolar crests (essential สำหรับ secondary crest formation); ventilation → scattered elastin ทั่วไป → elastin ไม่สามารถทำหน้าที่เป็น scaffold → arrested development → fewer, larger alveoli (ไม่มี fibrosis มาก). นี่คือ 'new BPD' morphology. ใน baboon model at 125d/186d GA: ventilated lung มี airspaces ขยายใหญ่กว่า 2.4 เท่า vs unventilated. การป้องกัน: CPAP ก่อน; LISA/INSURE; เลี่ยง MV ที่ไม่จำเป็น.

PEARL 4 — Fetal Lung Fluid Clearance: ENaC และ TTN
Fetal lung fluid ถูก cleared หลังคลอดด้วย 2 mechanisms ที่ทำงานต่างเวลากัน: (1) epinephrine ระหว่าง labor → stop secretion + เริ่ม reabsorption (แต่ mechanism นี้ ABSENT ในปอด preterm เว้นแต่จะ prime ด้วย corticosteroid + T3); (2) ENaC (epithelial sodium channel) → Na⁺-driven fluid absorption → ถ้า ENaC ทำงานไม่ได้ → fetal lung fluid ค้างอยู่ → respiratory failure. C-section → ไม่มี labor epinephrine surge → ↑ TTN risk. Glucocorticoids upregulate ENaC mRNA → อีกเหตุผลที่ ACS ช่วยได้ (ไม่ใช่แค่ surfactant). Preterm: lag ที่ compounding ทั้ง 2 mechanisms → ↑ TTN + RDS.

PEARL 5 — Chorioamnionitis → Lung Maturation: Additive กับ Steroids, ไม่ใช่ Contraindication
Chorioamnionitis/PPROM → lung maturation ผ่าน direct fetal lung contact with IL-1/LPS → modest inflammation/injury → resolves with ↑ surfactant + ↑ lung mechanics. ใน sheep model: intra-amniotic LPS gives MORE consistent + longer lung maturation than maternal corticosteroids. Importantly: ACS + chorioamnionitis → ADDITIVE maturation (Newnham 2001). Clinical consequence: PPROM หรือ chorioamnionitis ไม่ใช่ contraindication to ACS — ให้ยา. Concept เตือนใจ: 'preterm without RDS = has been stressed enough to mature'; 'preterm with RDS = normal unstressed preterm' — เตือนว่า RDS คือ default ของ preterm ถ้าไม่มี stressor.
10. Evidence Update — Key Studies
Avery ME, Mead J AMA J Dis Child 1959;97:517: first correlation of respiratory failure with ↓ surfactant in saline lung extracts of RDS infants — foundation of surfactant hypothesis
Gluck L et al Am J Obstet Gynecol 1971 (L/S ratio): lecithin-sphingomyelin ratio in amniotic fluid predicts lung immaturity; first clinical test of fetal lung maturity
Liggins GC, Howie RN Pediatrics 1972;50:515: first RCT antenatal glucocorticoid for prevention of RDS in preterm — ↓ RDS by ~50%; most impactful trial in neonatal history
Fujiwara T et al Lancet 1980;1:55: first successful surfactant treatment in humans — Surfacten (bovine lipid extract); proof of concept for surfactant therapy

Coalson JJ et al Am J Respir Crit Care Med 1999;160:1333: neonatal chronic lung disease in extremely immature baboons: arrested alveolar development with mechanical ventilation — established 'new BPD' morphology (arrested, not destructive)
Bland RD et al Am J Physiol 2008;294:L3: mechanical ventilation uncouples elastin synthesis/assembly → disrupts alveolar septation in newborn mice — mechanistic basis for ventilation-induced BPD
Roberts D et al Cochrane 2017;CD004454: antenatal corticosteroids for accelerating fetal lung maturation — ↓ RDS, ↓ IVH, ↓ NEC, ↓ neonatal mortality; confirms ACS as standard of care
Gyamfi-Bannerman C et al NEJM 2016;374:1311 (ALPS trial): single course betamethasone at 34+0–36+5 wk (late preterm): ↓ respiratory morbidity — supports ACS in late preterm period

Ninan K et al JAMA Pediatr 2022;176:e220483: meta-analysis: preterm ACS exposure → ↓ RDS mortality; at or near term: possible ↑ adverse neuropsychiatric disorders — benefit-risk ratio must be considered; indication for ACS may confound outcomes
Narayanan M et al AJRCCM 2012;185:186: hyperpolarized ³He MRI: alveolarization continues until at least age 20 — proof of concept for lung growth potential beyond childhood
Narayanan M et al AJRCCM 2013;187:1104: catch-up alveolarization in ex-preterm children at 10–14 yr with mild BPD — explains why some VLBW have normal lung function later

Stankiewicz P et al Am J Hum Genet 2009;84:780: FOXF1 deletions + mutations → alveolar capillary dysplasia with misaligned pulmonary veins — first molecular diagnosis of lethal congenital lung disorder
Mulrooney N et al AJRCCM 2005;171:488: surfactant pool >4 mg/kg → preterm lambs manageable with CPAP alone — physiologic basis for CPAP-first approach in RDS management
Kuypers E et al Am J Physiol 2012;302:L380: intra-amniotic LPS + antenatal betamethasone → additive lung maturation in preterm lambs — evidence base for not withholding ACS in PPROM/chorioamnionitis
Jobe AH, Bancalari E J Pediatr 2021;234:257: all-inclusive perspective on BPD: FEV1 trajectory at birth predicts adult lung function; ex-preterm at risk for early-onset COPD

Whitsett JA, Weaver TE NEJM 2002;347:2141: hydrophobic surfactant proteins (SP-B, SP-C) in lung function and disease — comprehensive review of SP-B/C structure, function, and genetic deficiency syndromes
Tyson JE et al NEJM 1999;340:1962 (Vitamin A): Vitamin A 5000 IU IM 3×/week × 4 wk in ELBW: ↓ BPD (OR 0.87, 95% CI 0.77–0.99) — stimulates alveolarization via retinoids
11. Self-Check Q&A
Q: จงบอก 5 stages ของ lung development พร้อม timing และ landmark event ของแต่ละ stage

ตอบ: (1) Embryonic (3–7 wk): lung bud → branching → lobar/segmental airways; vasculature from 6th aortic arch; failure → TEF/aplasia. (2) Pseudoglandular (5–17 wk): 15–20 gen airway branching complete; cartilage 9–10 wk; no gas exchange possible. (3) Canalicular (16–25 wk): viability transition — acinus, air-blood barrier (double→single cap), type II cells + lamellar bodies + surfactant. (4) Saccular (22 wk – term): terminal sacs branch/widen; alveolar crests with elastin by 28 wk; alveolarization starts ~32 wk. (5) Alveolar (32 wk – age 20): secondary crest formation; alveoli 50–150M at term → 500M adult; alveolarization continues into adulthood.
Q: Fetal lung fluid: ผลิตเท่าไหร่, clearance mechanism คืออะไร, และทำไม C-section ถึง ↑ TTN risk?

ตอบ: Production: ~4–5 mL/kg/hr → ~400 mL/day (3–4 kg fetus); intrinsic function lung epithelium; ไม่ขึ้นกับ vascular pressure หรือ fetal breathing. Clearance: (1) epinephrine during labor → stops secretion, starts Na⁺ reabsorption; (2) ENaC active Na⁺ transport → osmotic water clearance; most fluid → interstitial → pulmonary vasculature; <20% lymphatics. C-section ↑ TTN: no labor → no epinephrine surge → secretion-to-reabsorption switch not activated; ENaC still works but slower; ~35% residual fetal lung fluid must clear with breathing alone → delayed clearance → TTN. Preterm: BOTH mechanisms impaired (no epinephrine response + less ENaC).
Q: SP-B vs SP-C deficiency: ต่างกันอย่างไรทางพยาธิสรีรวิทยาและ clinical presentation?

ตอบ: SP-B deficiency (8 kDa; 121ins2 most common): lethal; term baby → respiratory failure from birth; loss of SP-B → abnormal lamellar bodies + unprocessed pro-SP-C in airspaces (diagnostic marker); animals with <20% SP-B → respiratory failure; treatment: lung transplant (surfactant = bridge only). SP-C deficiency (4 kDa; usually dominant negative processing mutation): mice lacking SP-C: normal at birth, develop progressive ILD + emphysema later; humans: familial ILD at variable onset age (not purely neonatal), NOT necessarily lethal in infancy; SP-B + SP-C cooperate for optimal surface film function. Key distinction: SP-B deficiency = neonatal lethal RDS; SP-C deficiency = progressive ILD (variable onset, adulthood possible).
Q: Glucocorticoids ช่วยปอดทารก preterm ผ่าน mechanisms อะไรบ้าง (ไม่ใช่แค่ surfactant)?

ตอบ: (1) Structural: ↑ surface area for gas exchange, ↓ mesenchyme thickness. (2) Type II cell: glycogen loss, ↑ fatty acid synthesis, ↑ beta-receptors, ↑ choline → sat-PC. (3) ↓ pulmonary edema: ↑ tight junction formation. (4) ENaC upregulation: → ↑ fetal lung fluid clearance (explaining TTN reduction). (5) ↑ SP-A: → ↑ innate host defense. (6) ↑ response to postnatal surfactant: ACS + surfactant > either alone (additive). (7) Late gestation mesenchyme: → ↑ matrix fibroblast differentiation → mature type II cells. (8) Extra-pulmonary: ↓ PDA, ↓ IVH, ↓ NEC, ↑ kidney tubular function.
Q: ABCA3 deficiency: gene function, clinical presentation, comparison to SP-B deficiency

ตอบ: ABCA3 gene: encodes ATP-binding cassette transporter localized to lamellar body membrane; functions as lipid transporter (loads phospholipids into lamellar bodies). Deficiency: most common genetic cause of lethal neonatal RDS (more common than SP-B deficiency); autosomal recessive; abnormal/absent lamellar bodies on EM; clinical presentation identical to SP-B deficiency (term infant with severe RDS). Mutation spectrum: severe 'null' mutations → neonatal lethal; some missense mutations → chronic ILD later. Diagnosis: genetic testing; EM of lung biopsy (small/abnormal lamellar bodies). Treatment: surfactant = bridge; lung transplant for severe cases; hydroxychloroquine or azithromycin for milder chronic forms (case reports).
Q: Alveolar capillary dysplasia (ACD): gene, pathology, presentation, management

ตอบ: Gene: FOXF1 (16q24.1); majority of cases; FOXF1 is transcription factor for lung mesenchyme development; Pathology: misaligned pulmonary veins located in same adventitial sheath as pulmonary artery (instead of separate interlobular septum); medial hypertrophy; capillary hypoplasia. Presentation: severe refractory PPHN from birth (term or near-term); 60–80% have extrapulmonary anomalies (cardiac, renal, GI); DOES NOT respond to iNO, sildenafil, ECMO. Diagnosis: lung biopsy (misaligned PVs on histology); FOXF1 sequencing (or panel). Prognosis: uniformly fatal without lung transplantation; ECMO as bridge to transplant evaluation.
Q: อธิบาย pressure-volume curve ของ surfactant-deficient lung และ effect ของ surfactant treatment

ตอบ: Surfactant-deficient preterm rabbit lung: (1) high opening pressure >25 cm H₂O (high surface tension in fluid menisci); (2) low maximal volume at 35 cm H₂O (many distal units still closed); (3) low deflation stability (collapses at low transpulmonary pressure → no FRC). After surfactant: (1) ↓ opening pressure → ~15 cm H₂O (lower surface tension → units open at lower pressures → more uniform inflation → less epithelial injury); (2) ↑ maximal volume ~2.5× (recruits alveolar gas volume); (3) retains ~40% volume at 5 cm H₂O (= FRC equivalent). Clinical translation: rapid ↑ oxygenation (↑ lung volume) after surfactant before Pco₂/compliance improve (more gradual).
Q: จงอธิบาย 4 types ของ congenital lung malformations ที่เจอบ่อยใน NICU

ตอบ: CCAM/CPAM: multicystic overproliferation; communicates with tracheobronchial tree; normal blood supply; unilobar; ↑ risk pleuropulmonary blastoma; surgical resection. Bronchogenic cyst: single cyst; mediastinum most common; lung parenchyma form: NO communication with tracheobronchial tree; causes airway compression/recurrent pneumonia; resect. Congenital lobar emphysema: hyperinflation of 1+ lobes; left upper or right middle lobe; mediastinal shift; surgical resection if severe. Pulmonary sequestration: no bronchial communication; systemic artery supply; intralobar (intrathoracic, childhood) vs extralobar (own pleura, prenatal/neonatal); resection or embolization.
Q: ทำไม preterm infant ที่เกิดจาก chorioamnionitis บางคนถึงไม่มี RDS?

ตอบ: Chorioamnionitis-induced lung maturation: IL-1 และ LPS (จาก bacteria หรือ endo/exotoxins) → direct contact กับ fetal lung → activates innate immune response → modest controlled inflammation → ↑ surfactant lipid + protein pool sizes + ↑ lung gas volumes (Kuypers 2012). Effect duration: longer and more consistent than maternal corticosteroids in animal models. Additive กับ ACS: steroids suppress inflammation initially but amplify later response via matured innate immunity. Clinical concept: 'preterm without RDS = has been stressed (including by intrauterine infection) enough to mature'; PPROM ≠ contraindication to ACS — ให้ยา. Challenge: chorioamnionitis also contributes to BPD via macrophage priming (↑ inflammatory state at delivery).
Q: Surfactant pool size: RDS vs term; และ alveolar life cycle ของ surfactant คืออะไร?

ตอบ: Pool sizes: RDS infants: ~5 mg/kg; preterm lambs: CPAP feasible if >4 mg/kg; term infants: ~100 mg/kg; exogenous treatment dose > endogenous needed (non-uniform distribution of treatment). Alveolar life cycle: lamellar bodies secreted → unravel → tubular myelin (SP-A at corners + SP-B + phospholipids; = reserve pool in hypophase) → surface film (area compression → concentrate sat-PC, squeeze out unsaturated lipid) → 'used' surfactant = small vesicles (little SP-A/B/C) → endocytosis → multivesicular bodies → lamellar bodies → re-secreted. Recycling: 90% phospholipids recycled intact in term/preterm lung (vs ~50% in adult); slow catabolism → treatment dose stays in lung days. In preterm: faster conversion surface-active → inactive forms → pulmonary edema accelerates depletion of active fraction.

Day 161 Preview — Fanaroff Ch.64
บทต่อไป: Chapter 64 — Assessment of Neonatal Pulmonary Function. เตรียมตัว: pulmonary function testing (PFT) ใน neonates: techniques, reference values, interpretation; lung mechanics — compliance, resistance, time constants; respiratory inductance plethysmography, forced expiration, tidal breathing analysis; FRC measurement (He dilution, SF₆ washout, body plethysmography); airway resistance ใน BPD vs RDS; bedside monitoring: flow-volume loops, work of breathing assessment; clinical application ในการ wean ventilation และ predict extubation success.
